title: A Day in the Life of a Flight Student
body: PSA: This is NOT what flight training looks like at all. That is flight training at a part 141 academy that takes things WAY to seriously. Training part 61 is as simple as showing up, and flying a plane. A decent 141 school is showing up, and flying the plane, along with separate days doing ground classes and other college courses for your degree. Only bad academy’s force students to do their ground studies before flying. Any decent school will have EVERY student in the air flying planes within their first week at school.
author: @DaCatmasterX

title: A Day in the Life of a Flight Student
body: PPL is affordable. CPL is where the money problem starts. I’m 32 and will be embarking on my flying journey this year after working for a decade saving up money.

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body:
Implied by, but not mentioned in, this conversation: What Is Ancient Philosophy? by Pierre Hadot (1995), dealing with philosophy as a way of life rather than an academic / research discipline.

A liberal education is similar: its pursuit can be a way of life. An incredibly challenging and rewarding one, with its own skills and ways.

When the guest expressed her love for the subject of philosophy, the enthusiasm and genuineness of it brought tears to my eyes.

Thank you for the conversation.
author: @j3kfd9j

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: At about 36 minutes, Prof. Frey, whose zeal for pure learning I deeply admire, argues that it is patronizing to say students don't want to read. But I am a professor and I am here to say that they often don't. Perhaps reading itself is reverting to the earlier oral culture, but in any event, their reading skills are often rudimentary, although there is usually a small cadre of achievers who read very well, as well as any older people.
author: @tasilovonheydebrandtundder6851

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: Great conversation. I do think Dr. Frey's observations come from a self-selecting population; that is, the kind of student who wants to sign up for an honors college is probably also the kind of student who wants to read a lot and engage in heavy discussion.
author: @Threeve703

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: Hooray for liberal education and liberal arts—the arts of freedom! And thanks to these folks for supporting and advocating for them. Appreciate the emphasis on intellectual virtues (excellences), but even more courage is called for because a real liberal education also does not shy from the moral virtues. Even for Aristotle, the human was a union of both sorts of excellence: whole human goodness demands head AND heart.
author: @JosephDyer-s9u

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: Where does one go when the system closes the door? Can a liberal education be pursued in this college environment? Is the future simply one of sequestration?
author: @S.ThomasAllred

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: This was a great discussion, but I want to challenge one of your assumptions. In your description, you mention “liberal” education versus “servile” education. In addition, your guest said that “philosophers can do anything.” I want to assert that a professional education is not the same thing as “education for trades” that Aristotle had in mind. Engineering or Law are very complex intellectual endeavors, they are not olive tree farming or weaving cloth. I think it is common sense that a philosopher cannot  do state of the art engineering or surgery or molecular biology without formal training in that discipline.
author: @artscience9981

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: 95% of this interview is about the curriculum Dr. Frey built -- which is interesting, but I was really looking forward to hearing how the University killed her program "as quickly as you might imagine." She never gets a chance to explain as the host puts it at 7:23, "exactly how it ended."

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body:
@20:33 "something that looks kind of like science" ???

I started reading Science Fiction when I was 9 years old. It was not suggested or encouraged by any adults.

In 7th grade I read:

A Fall of Moondust by Arthur C Clarke

he used Plato's Allegory of the Cave to explain the perception of reality via infrared.

School was relatively uninteresting and uninformative compared to Hard SF. Not junk like Star Wars!

Compare:

The Dispossessed by Ursula K LeGuin

to:

Voyage from Yesteryear by James P Hogan

I decided to go to college for engineering in 7th grade and went for electrical engineering. I was amazed at how many 18 year olds had no idea what they wanted to do.

Produce pseudo-intellectual BS for more people to not read. Who cares what PostModernism is? Was the Moon 🌘🌕🌒 modern enough.

Read A Fall of Moondust. Is that pre-modern?
author: @psikeyhackr6914

title: This University Built an Honors College — and Then Destroyed It | The Honest Broker #5
body: She say, "We don’t need experts in metaphysics, we really don’t.” This is woefully naive. Countering transhumanism? Understanding the limits of AI? Growing out of the outworn philosophy of scientific materialism? All of these important contemporary challenges require expertise in metaphysics in order to be intelligently addressed.
